evals.expected_output: Single-file HTML teaching document delegating presentation to html-document (no redefined CSS already in elements.md). Includes: document type breadcrumb, title + audience, concept map, one lesson with Java equivalent + concept + Java mental model callout + strategy + before/after stacked panels + line-by-line walkthrough + concept callouts for async, await, optional chaining ?., nullish coalescing ?? with Java parallel and gotcha, gotchas list, verification. Because the callback-to-async control-flow shift is a genuinely hard concept, the lesson also includes an optional 'Explain Like I'm Five' section (rendered as an html-document .callout, no new CSS) with a single jargon-free, code-free everyday analogy for async/await suspension — distinct from, not a restatement of, the Concept and Java Mental Model. Print-friendly.

Single-file HTML teaching document deferring presentation to html-document. Includes lesson with Java mental model mapping @dataclass(frozen=True) to a Java record / immutable POJO, concept callouts for the decorator, default args, frozen-instance __post_init__ workaround, optional type `int | None`. Before/after stacked panels via html-document elements catalog. Gotchas covering mutable default args, frozen mutation trick. Verification step. No 'Explain Like I'm Five' section: a frozen dataclass maps cleanly onto the Java record / immutable POJO the reader already knows, so an everyday analogy would patronise and bloat — the skill scopes ELI5 to genuinely hard concepts only.

Single-file HTML teaching document deferring presentation to html-document (no redefined CSS). One lesson with Java equivalent (a private static final constant), concept, Java mental model, strategy (readability / single source of truth), before/after stacked panels, line-by-line walkthrough, verification. A concept callout for the numeric separator `30_000` is reasonable; module-level `const` maps 1:1 to `static final` and needs little. Crucially, NO 'Explain Like I'm Five' section appears: extracting a named constant is something a Java engineer groks instantly, so the skill must omit ELI5 here rather than force a trivial analogy. Print-friendly.
